text: Hello, good morning. This is a case of the total laparoscopic hysterectomy and bilateral salpingo-ophorectomy.

The surgeon uses a ligature device to resect the round ligament approximately four centimeters lateral to the uterus, employing a 37-millimeter ligature and an atraumatic grasp through the ipsilateral port. The fallopian tube, located approximately three centimeters lateral to the uterus, is desiccated and dissected using the ligature.

This is a forty-two year old female with the DUV and she has a severe bleeding. That's why we are performing. Family is complete. In peri-monoparsal age group, the bleeding is sometimes not controlled by the medicine, so she has opted for the total laparoscopic hysterectomy.

We will do the sulpangiofractomy at the end after removal of the uterus. There are two ways to do the BSO. Either you can take it from the infandlo-pulvic ligament in the beginning or at the end you can remove it before closure of the vault.
The surgeon has separated the right adnexal structures and is preparing to proceed with the separation of the left adnexal structures. The Misraz knot will be used for ligating the uterine artery.

That is a very good extracorporeal knot that can be used with the confidence for the purpose of securing the artery. In this patient, total blood loss was just approximately ten ml and we have not used any suction irrigation throughout the surgery.

The surgeon separates the omentum adhered to the fimbrial end of the left fallopian tube. The ovarian ligament is visible, and the previously cut fallopian tubes are observed two centimeters lateral. The surgeon cleans the jaws of the ligature device to enhance its efficiency and improve tissue response.

The surgeon identifies the ovarian ligament and completes the dissection of the left adnexal structures. Using a harmonic scalpel, the anterior leaf of the broad ligament is separated. The harmonic scalpel vibrates at fifty-five thousand vibrations per second, breaking hydrogen bonds to create a clear dissection plane for the UV fold. A grasper lifts the peritoneum anteriorly to prevent damage from the harmonic scalpel, while the assistant manipulates the uterus from twelve o'clock to five o'clock and seven o'clock positions, facilitating anterior traction of the peritoneum to reveal a good areolar plane for separation.

The surgeon separates the peritoneum and begins dissecting the bladder using a gauze piece fashioned into a peanut. The uterus is retroverted using a uterine manipulator, exposing the vaginal fascia. The bladder muscles are pushed upwards with the gauze, achieving a bloodless field by minimizing bleeding.

The uterus is positioned in an anteverted orientation, and the surgeon uses the active jaw of the harmonic device to apply traction and separate the posterior peritoneum at the junction of the uterosacral ligament and cervix. The vaginal part of the uterosacral ligament is preserved during this dissection.

The surgeon completes the separation of the posterior peritoneum on the right side, starting from the posterior leaf of the broad ligament and extending up to the cervix. The peritoneum is spread to define the angle for knot placement. The needle pierces the defined point to tie the uterine bundle with a knot, minimizing blood loss without relying on energy sources. The uterine artery is clearly visible on both sides, and a portion of the broad ligament on the left side is also separated.

The surgeon uses a 30 mm endoskeletal needle to take a bite from posterior to anterior, while the assistant retroverts the uterus. The needle tip is visible anteriorly, and the suture is fed through, allowing the needle to be safely extracted. Outside the body, the surgeon ties Misra's knot, creating a dumbbell effect on the uterine bundle. The left side of the uterus begins to discolor, indicating successful ligation, and the same procedure is repeated on the right side.

The surgeon uses an intracorporeal suture technique on the right side, employing a knot pusher to secure the Misra's knot without cutting. The knot configuration includes multiple hitches, winds, and locks, forming a secure dumbbell shape that effectively ligates the vein and artery, preventing bleeding. The uterus appears paler as the blood supply is halted. The knot pusher is removed, and a ligature is applied distal to the knot to cut the uterine vessel.

The surgeon transfers the ligature to the right side, positioning the tip at a right angle to the uterine vessel. The uterine artery is secured with a knot, and the illumination reveals no bleeding from the artery.

The surgeon uses a colpotomizer to perform an anterior colpotomy with a harmonic scalpel. The tip of the harmonic is positioned within the ring of the colpotomizer to prevent lateral movement, while the assistant provides support. The harmonic scalpel is preferred for its ability to achieve hemostasis and avoid electrosurgical injuries. The active jaw of the harmonic is used to cut the left side of the colpotomy, with the uterine manipulator ring becoming visible during the procedure.

The assistant applies traction while the surgeon uses the harmonic scalpel to cut the vaginal fascia and epithelium, with the cervix visible. The plastic tip of the colpotomizer is also visible. The anterior colpotomy is completed, and the harmonic is transferred to the opposite side to finish the colpotomy. The posterior colpotomy is performed without cutting the uterosacral ligament, using a three-degree telescope for better visualization. The colpotomizer is fully inside the abdomen, allowing the uterus to be delivered through the vagina.

The surgeon performs a left salpingo-oophorectomy using a ligature device, applying gentle anteromedial traction to ensure hemostasis. The ovary and fallopian tube are removed and pushed into the vagina. The procedure is repeated on the right side.

The surgeon performs the right side salpingo-oophorectomy using a ligature device, ensuring the instrument's jaw is at a right angle to the target tissue for optimal hemostasis. Gentle endometrial traction is applied to maintain distance from the iliac vessels, and the ovary and fallopian tube are subsequently pushed into the vagina.

The surgeon begins suturing the vaginal vault, ensuring the utero sacral ligament remains intact. Using an endoskeletal needle with number one fibril, two bites are taken on each corner, incorporating the anterior and posterior vaginal fascia and epithelium to prevent granulation tissue formation. The assistant pulls the peritoneum anteriorly during this process. The surgeon uses an extra corporeal square knot technique, securing the sutures with at least three knots to pull the utero sacral angles closer together.
And the total blood loss is less than ten ml. So thank you very much for watching this video. This was just a simple case of TLH plus Bilactin-Salt-N-Geo-Fetamine. Have a nice day.
